blocks.text: Sincerity, or Ikhlas, is the soul of our deeds. In a world that often values visibility and recognition, Ikhlas calls us to a private, pure connection with Allah. It is the practice of performing every act of worship solely for His pleasure, stripping away the desire for human approval or worldly status. Without sincerity, our actions are like dust in the wind; with it, even the smallest deed can weigh heavily on the scales of the Hereafter.

Ikhlas is derived from the Arabic word meaning 'to be pure' or 'to be refined.' Just as milk is refined from other substances, our worship must be refined from the impurities of 'Riya' (showing off) and 'Sum'ah' (seeking reputation). True sincerity means that your private worship is as diligent as your public worship, and that you are not discouraged when no one notices your good deeds, for you know that Allah is the All-Seeing.

blocks.textUthmani: إِنَّآ أَنزَلۡنَآ إِلَيۡكَ ٱلۡكِتَٰبَ بِٱلۡحَقِّ فَٱعۡبُدِ ٱللَّهَ مُخۡلِصٗا لَّهُ ٱلدِّينَ
blocks.translationEn: It is We who sent down the Scripture to you [Prophet] with the Truth, so worship God with your total devotion

blocks.text: This verse reminds us that the revelation itself was sent down in truth, and its primary demand is that we worship Allah with 'Mukhlis'—sincere devotion. This sincerity is not an optional extra; it is the very essence of the religion that Allah has prescribed for us.

blocks.textEn: Narrated 'Umar bin Al-Khattab: I heard Allah's Messenger (ﷺ) saying, "The reward of deeds depends upon the intentions and every person will get the reward according to what he has intended. So whoever emigrated for worldly benefits or for a woman to marry, his emigration was for what he emigrated for

blocks.text: Although this hadith is the foundation for intention, it is also the ultimate test of sincerity. If the 'migration' of our heart is truly toward Allah, then our deeds are accepted. If our heart is migrating toward a worldly benefit, then that benefit is all we will receive. Sincerity is the 'engine' that determines the final destination of our actions.

The scholars warn that sincerity is a constant struggle. Ibn al-Mubarak said, 'How many a small deed is made great by its intention, and how many a great deed is made small by its intention!' Always be wary of the subtle desire for others to see you in a state of devotion.

To cultivate Ikhlas, try to keep some of your good deeds completely secret. Whether it's an extra prayer at night or a hidden act of charity, having a 'secret' with Allah builds a foundation of sincerity that will protect you when you worship in public.

When we stand in prayer, Ikhlas allows us to forget the people around us. We are not praying for the person next to us or the imam; we are standing before the Lord of the Worlds. This realization is the key to unlocking true Khushu.
